audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Ruth threshing-floor plan, nighttime encounter, wing/redeemer request, nearer redeemer, grain gift, and waiting narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Naomi's rest/security aim, threshing-floor setting, washing/anointing/clothing instructions, feet/place euphemism question, Ruth's nighttime vulnerability, direct self-identification, spread-your-wing request, hesed and age/power language, eshet-hayil recognition, nearer-redeemer procedure, oath, secrecy, six-barley measure, pronoun variant, and Naomi's final counsel.

verses.restored: Naomi her mother-in-law said to her, My daughter, should I not seek rest for you, that it may be well with you?
Now, is not Boaz our kinsman, with whose young women you were? Look, he is winnowing barley tonight at threshing floor.
Wash yourself, anoint yourself, put your garments upon you, and go down to threshing floor. Do not make yourself known to man until he has finished eating and drinking.
When he lies down, know place where he lies. Go, uncover place of his feet, and lie down. He will tell you what you shall do.
She said to her, All that you say to me, I will do.
She went down to threshing floor and did according to all that her mother-in-law commanded her.
Boaz ate and drank, and his heart was good. He went to lie at end of grain heap. She came quietly, uncovered place of his feet, and lay down.
At middle of night, the man trembled and turned himself, and look—a woman was lying at place of his feet.
He said, Who are you? She said, I am Ruth, your servant. Spread your wing over your servant, because you are a redeemer.
He said, Blessed are you by YHWH, my daughter. You have made your latter covenant-kindness better than first by not going after young men, whether poor or rich.
Now, my daughter, do not fear. All that you say I will do for you, because all gate of my people knows that you are a woman of strength and worth.
Now, indeed, it is true that I am a redeemer, but there is also a redeemer nearer than I.
Stay this night. In morning, if he will redeem you, good—let him redeem. But if he does not desire to redeem you, then I will redeem you, as YHWH lives. Lie down until morning.
She lay at place of his feet until morning and arose before one could recognize another. He said, Let it not be known that the woman came to threshing floor.
He said, Bring cloak that is upon you and hold it. She held it, and he measured six measures of barley and placed it upon her. Then he went into city.
She came to her mother-in-law, who said, How did it go with you, my daughter? She told her all that the man had done for her.
She said, These six measures of barley he gave to me, because he said to me, Do not go empty to your mother-in-law.
Naomi said, Sit, my daughter, until you know how matter will fall, because man will not rest unless he completes matter today.

translation_notes: Naomi seeks 'rest' or secure settlement for Ruth within the limited economic and marital structures available to widows. The goal is safety, not a declaration that every woman needs marriage.

The threshing floor is a nighttime workplace with food, drink, sleeping workers, and sexual ambiguity. Ruth enters a vulnerable setting; the scene must not become a universal dating, seduction, or obedience script.
'Feet' may be literal and can function euphemistically elsewhere. The Hebrew does not explicitly narrate intercourse. Translation should preserve ambiguity without sensationalizing or denying risk.
Ruth does more than obey Naomi: she identifies herself, names Boaz as redeemer, and directly asks him to spread his wing or garment over her. She articulates a kinship and economic claim.
The wing request echoes Boaz's blessing in 2:12. Divine refuge is now pressed into human responsibility: Boaz is asked to embody the protection he invoked.
Boaz's praise of Ruth as a woman of hayil—strength, worth, capability—matches the description applied to him in 2:1. It is not sexual-purity grading.

Boaz is older, wealthier, male, locally established, and able to control legal access. Ruth's courage does not erase this power imbalance or make silence and compliance equivalent to unrestricted consent.

Boaz acknowledges a nearer redeemer rather than privately taking control. The legal process protects competing family claims, though the system still treats women's futures through male-governed transactions.
His request for secrecy may protect Ruth's reputation and the legal process, but secrecy in a vulnerable nighttime encounter must never be idealized as a requirement in modern relationships.
The six measures of barley are materially significant and intentionally answer Naomi's language of emptiness. Grain is provision, not payment for sexual access.
Verse 15 has a Hebrew pronoun variation reflected in witnesses and traditions—whether he or she went into city. Later textual audit should compare the Masoretic reading and ancient versions.
safety_note: Do not use Ruth 3 to teach sexual compliance, covert encounters, age or wealth imbalance as romance, or obedience to elders over bodily autonomy. Preserve Ruth's initiative, the scene's ambiguity, Boaz's procedural restraint, and the difference between covenant responsibility and possession.
